aliases: Steamed milk custard with double skin

english: Steamed milk custard with double skin

region_of_origin: Guangdong, China (Cantonese origin); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for double-layer steamed milk custard (Shuang Pi Nai). FOODON_03301710 (milk-based dessert food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Milk-based desserts) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for Shuang Pi Nai / double-layer steamed milk custard in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for double-layer steamed milk custard or Shuang Pi Nai. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual dessert preparations at this specificity.

preparation_method: rested at room temperature to form milk skin
double-layered by pouring cooled milk back over reformed skin

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of double-layer milk custard is approximately 200 g, typically served in a ceramic bowl or cup as a dessert or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Shuang Pi Nai (Double-skin steamed milk pudding) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central whole milk and egg white components, cross-referenced with published Cantonese dessert composition data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy); supplementary reference: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Shuang Pi Nai / double-layer milk custard exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the standard recipe composition: approximately 85–90% whole fresh milk (USDA FDC #170860, whole milk 3.25% fat) and 10–15% egg white (USDA FDC #173423), with approximately 6–8 g added sugar per 100 g of finished product. The resulting per-100g profile is consistent with published values for similar Cantonese steamed milk desserts in FAO/INFOODS SEA regional tables. Confidence is medium due to recipe-based estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based desserts and dairy puddings — analogous estimate based on whole milk GI (GI=31–34) and added sugar contribution; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shuang Pi Nai / double-layer steamed milk custard in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 40 (low category) based on: (1) whole milk GI of 31–34 (University of Sydney GI Database, tested value); (2) egg white contribution (negligible GI effect); (3) moderate added sugar (~11.8 g/100g) raising the blended GI above plain milk. This is consistent with the GI range of 35–45 reported for similar milk-based puddings and custards in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 12.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 10.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~58 mg/100g; ~116 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 200 g serving contributes approximately 116 mg sodium (about 5% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), making it acceptable for most hypertensive individuals when consumed occasionally and without high-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~11.8 g sugar/100g) and moderate glycemic index (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 23.6 g of sugar per 200 g serving and a GI of ~40, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and monitor postprandial glucose response, particularly if consuming alongside other carbohydrate-rich foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g/100g) from whole milk; low trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~4.2 g per 200 g serving) is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dessert in moderation as part of a diet that keeps total saturated fat below 10% of daily energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double-Layer Milk Custard, known in Cantonese as Shuang Pi Nai (双皮奶 / 雙皮奶), is a traditional Cantonese steamed milk dessert originating from Shunde, Guangdong Province, China. It is made from whole fresh milk, egg whites, and sugar. The preparation involves heating fresh milk until a skin forms on the surface, then carefully pouring the milk out while leaving the skin intact in the bowl. The milk is mixed with beaten egg whites and sugar, then gently poured back over the reserved skin. The bowl is covered and steamed gently until the custard sets, forming a second skin on top — hence the name 'double layer.' The result is a silky, smooth, lightly sweet custard with a delicate, slightly chewy milk skin on both the top and bottom. The texture is softer and more delicate than Western egg custard, with a pronounced fresh-milk aroma. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with 4.8 g protein, 3.5 g fat, and 12.5 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy dessert. It is a good source of calcium (~145 mg/100g) and riboflavin from the milk base. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low GI), though the added sugar (~11.8 g/100g) means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size. It is gluten-free and vegetarian. In Singapore, it is commonly served in Cantonese dessert shops (糖水铺) as a warm or chilled dessert.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat double-layer milk custard?
What is the GI of milk custard?
Where to find 双皮奶 in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Shuang Pi Nai / double-layer steamed milk custard was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the standard recipe composition using USDA FDC whole milk and egg white entries, with added sugar adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for whole milk and similar milk-based puddings. Confidence is medium rather than low because the recipe composition is well-established and the ingredient-level data sources are high quality.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are recipe-estimated from USDA FDC whole milk (FDC #170860) and egg white (FDC #173423) components; no exact composite dish entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FDC. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as the dish contains no plant-derived fibre sources (milk, egg white, and sugar contain no dietary fibre). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero at trace levels below detection threshold in this preparation; whole milk contains only negligible naturally-occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) below 0.05 g/100g. 4. GI is an analogous estimate of 40 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database whole milk values and Atkinson et al. (2008) milk-based pudding data; no direct GI measurement for Shuang Pi Nai was found. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC whole milk and egg white component data; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. 6. Food group corrected from input 'Beverages' to 'Desserts' as Shuang Pi Nai is a steamed custard dessert, not a beverage. 7. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (milk, egg white, sugar) with no pork or alcohol; formal halal certification for the specific restaurant was not verified.
